books.title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things
books.author: Ben Horowitz
books.note: No platitudes, no frameworks — just honest accounts of what it actually feels like to lead through chaos. The section on layoffs alone is worth the read.

books.title: Blue Ocean Strategy
books.author: Renée Mauborgne & W. Chan Kim
books.note: Reframes competition entirely — instead of fighting over existing demand, create new market space. Still working through it.

books.title: Launch in 5
books.author: Will Russel
books.note: Practical and fast. Cuts through the overthinking that kills most side projects before they start.

books.title: Supercommunicators
books.author: Charles Duhigg
books.note: The research on conversational loops and matching the type of conversation — practical, emotional, or social — changed how I approach 1:1s and design discussions.

books.title: The 12 Week Year
books.author: Brian P. Moran & Michael Lennington
books.note: Compressing annual goals into 12-week cycles creates urgency without burnout. I restructured my side project schedule around this.

books.title: Deep Work
books.author: Cal Newport
books.note: Made me audit how much of my day was shallow. The time-blocking approach stuck — I use it every week now.

books.title: The Art of Statistics
books.author: David Spiegelhalter
books.note: Statistics explained through real cases rather than formulas. Gave me a much clearer framework for interpreting A/B test results and system metrics.

books.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
books.author: Martin Kleppmann
books.note: The definitive book on distributed systems and data engineering. Changed how I think about databases, replication, and consistency.

books.title: The Productivity Project
books.author: Chris Bailey
books.note: A year-long productivity experiment distilled into actionable principles. The three ingredients — time, attention, energy — reframed how I think about output.

books.title: Cloud Native Patterns
books.author: Cornelia Davis
books.note: Practical patterns for building resilient cloud-native services. Directly applicable to microservices work on AWS.

books.title: Kubernetes in Action
books.author: Marko Luksa
books.note: Essential reading for the CKA. Goes deep on pods, services, and cluster internals in a way that actually sticks.

books.title: Clean Architecture
books.author: Robert C. Martin
books.note: Revisiting this with fresh eyes after working with DDD in production. A lot more hits now.

papers.title: Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store
papers.note: The paper behind DynamoDB. Explains consistent hashing, vector clocks, and eventual consistency better than any textbook I've found.

papers.title: MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters
papers.note: The paper that made large-scale parallelism feel tractable. Fundamental reading for anyone working with cloud-scale data pipelines.

papers.title: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Raft)
papers.note: Required reading after getting my CKA. Understanding Raft made etcd and Kubernetes leader election finally click.

papers.title: Serverless Computing: One Step Forward, Two Steps Back
papers.note: A balanced critique of the serverless model. Made me think harder about when Lambda is the right tool and when it isn't.
